title.en: Qwen3-VL-2B: Build a Local Multimodal Pipeline for Document and Scene Analysis

description.en: Chain Qwen3-VL-2B-Instruct into a reusable multimodal pipeline that classifies images, extracts structured data from documents, and routes visual tasks — all locally with zero API costs.

story_variants.en:
## From Single Prompts to Visual Pipelines

Vaathiyaar here! You already know Qwen3-VL-2B can look at an image and describe it. But a single image-to-text call is just the beginning. What if you could build a **pipeline** that classifies the type of image, extracts structured data, and routes each visual task to the right handler — all running locally on your hardware? That is what separates a demo from a real assistant.

### The Pipeline Architecture

A visual pipeline chains multiple inference calls, each narrowing the task:

| Stage | Purpose | Example Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Classify** | Determine image type | `"receipt"`, `"screenshot"`, `"photo"` |
| **Extract** | Pull structured data | `{"total": "$42.50", "date": "2026-04-19"}` |
| **Summarize** | Generate human-readable output | `"Grocery receipt for $42.50 from..."` |

Each stage uses the same Qwen3-VL-2B model but with a different system prompt — no extra models needed.

### Step 1: Build the Classification Stage

```python
from transformers import Qwen3VLForConditionalGeneration, AutoProcessor
from PIL import Image

model_name = "Qwen/Qwen3-VL-2B-Instruct"
processor = AutoProcessor.from_pretrained(model_name)
model = Qwen3VLForConditionalGeneration.from_pretrained(
    model_name, torch_dtype="auto", device_map="auto"
)

def classify_image(image: Image.Image) -> str:
    messages = [
        {"role": "system", "content": "Classify the image into exactly one category: receipt, screenshot, chart, photo, document. Reply with only the category word."},
        {"role": "user", "content": [
            {"type": "image", "image": image},
            {"type": "text", "text": "What type of image is this?"}
        ]}
    ]
    prompt = processor.apply_chat_template(
        messages, tokenize=False, add_generation_prompt=True
    )
    inputs = processor(text=[prompt], images=[image],
                       return_tensors="pt").to(model.device)
    output = model.generate(**inputs, max_new_tokens=10, temperature=0.1)
    return processor.decode(
        output[0][inputs["input_ids"].shape[-1]:],
        skip_special_tokens=True
    ).strip().lower()
```

Low temperature (0.1) and a strict system prompt force a one-word classification — no rambling.

### Step 2: Route to a Specialized Extractor

```python
EXTRACTOR_PROMPTS = {
    "receipt": "Extract: store name, date, items with prices, total. Return as key: value lines.",
    "chart": "Describe the chart type, axes, trends, and key data points.",
    "screenshot": "Extract all visible text. Identify UI elements and their state.",
    "document": "Extract the title, headings, and main body text.",
    "photo": "Describe the scene, subjects, setting, and notable details.",
}

def extract_data(image: Image.Image, category: str) -> str:
    system_prompt = EXTRACTOR_PROMPTS.get(category, EXTRACTOR_PROMPTS["photo"])
    messages = [
        {"role": "system", "content": system_prompt},
        {"role": "user", "content": [
            {"type": "image", "image": image},
            {"type": "text", "text": "Analyze this image."}
        ]}
    ]
    prompt = processor.apply_chat_template(
        messages, tokenize=False, add_generation_prompt=True
    )
    inputs = processor(text=[prompt], images=[image],
                       return_tensors="pt").to(model.device)
    output = model.generate(**inputs, max_new_tokens=300, temperature=0.3)
    return processor.decode(
        output[0][inputs["input_ids"].shape[-1]:],
        skip_special_tokens=True
    ).strip()
```

### Step 3: The Full Pipeline

```python
def analyze_image(image_path: str) -> dict:
    image = Image.open(image_path).convert("RGB")
    category = classify_image(image)
    extraction = extract_data(image, category)
    return {
        "path": image_path,
        "category": category,
        "analysis": extraction
    }

result = analyze_image("receipt.jpg")
print(f"Type: {result['category']}")
print(f"Analysis:\n{result['analysis']}")
```

### Batching Multiple Images

Process a folder of images and collect structured results:

```python
import glob

def batch_analyze(folder: str) -> list:
    results = []
    for path in glob.glob(f"{folder}/*.jpg") + glob.glob(f"{folder}/*.png"):
        results.append(analyze_image(path))
    return results
```

### Performance Tips

| Technique | Effect | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| `torch.float16` | Halves VRAM | Always on GPU |
| `max_new_tokens=10` | Faster classification | Classification stage only |
| `temperature=0.1` | Deterministic output | Classification / extraction |
| Resize to 448px | Faster processing | When fine detail is not needed |
| `torch.no_grad()` | Saves memory | Always during inference |

> **Key Insight:** A single 2B-parameter model can power an entire visual analysis pipeline when you vary the system prompt per stage. Classification, extraction, and summarization are not three models — they are three prompts. Design your pipeline around *prompt routing*, and Qwen3-VL-2B becomes a local multimodal Swiss army knife.

animation_sequence.content: A single image-to-text call is a demo. A pipeline that classifies, extracts, and routes visual tasks is an assistant. Qwen3-VL-2B-Instruct can power all three stages locally — same model, different system prompts, structured output.

steps.explanation: Import the Qwen3-VL model class and AutoProcessor — together they handle both text tokenization and image pixel preprocessing.
Point to the Hugging Face model ID. The 2B-Instruct variant is instruction-tuned for chat and fits in ~5GB VRAM.
Load model weights with automatic dtype and device placement. On a GPU machine, layers land on CUDA automatically.
Define classify_image — the first pipeline stage. It takes a PIL Image and returns a single category string.
The system prompt constrains the model to reply with exactly one word from a fixed set — this turns a generative model into a classifier.
The user message combines an image block and a text block. Qwen3-VL's processor knows how to interleave image tokens with text tokens.
apply_chat_template converts the message list into the special-token format the model was fine-tuned on.
max_new_tokens=10 and temperature=0.1 — we want a single deterministic word, not a paragraph. Fast and focused.
Decode only the newly generated tokens (skip the input prompt) and normalize to lowercase for reliable downstream routing.
A dictionary maps each image category to a specialized system prompt — this is the routing table that makes one model behave like five.
extract_data takes the classified category and selects the matching prompt. Unknown categories fall back to the generic 'photo' handler.
The system prompt changes per category, but the user message structure stays identical — same image block, same trigger text.
max_new_tokens=300 gives room for detailed extraction. temperature=0.3 balances accuracy with natural phrasing.
analyze_image ties the pipeline together: open image, classify it, extract data, return a structured dict with path, category, and analysis.
convert('RGB') ensures consistency — some PNGs have alpha channels that would cause preprocessing errors.

items.left: One generic prompt for all images

items.right: Dict output ready for downstream code

items.left: Same quality for receipts and photos

animation_sequence.message: You have built a local multimodal pipeline! Qwen3-VL-2B classifies images, routes to specialized extractors, and returns structured data — all on your hardware with zero API costs.

instruction.en: Build an `ImagePipeline` class that simulates a multi-stage visual analysis pipeline. It should accept a dictionary of category-to-prompt mappings on init, then process images through two stages: `classify(image_path)` determines the category by checking if any category keyword appears in the filename (case-insensitive), defaulting to 'photo' if none match. `extract(image_path, category)` returns a dict with 'category', 'prompt_used' (from the mapping), and 'output' (a string: 'Extracted {category} data from {filename}'). Finally, `run(image_path)` chains classify then extract and returns the full result dict. Track all processed paths in a `history` list.

practice_challenges.starter_code:
class ImagePipeline:
    def __init__(self, prompts: dict):
        # Store prompts mapping and initialize history list
        pass

    def classify(self, image_path: str) -> str:
        # Check if any key from self.prompts appears in the
        # filename (case-insensitive). Return the matching
        # category or 'photo' as default.
        pass

    def extract(self, image_path: str, category: str) -> dict:
        # Return dict with:
        #   'category': the category string
        #   'prompt_used': self.prompts.get(category, 'Describe the image.')
        #   'output': f'Extracted {category} data from {filename}'
        # where filename is just the file name, not the full path
        pass

    def run(self, image_path: str) -> dict:
        # 1. classify the image
        # 2. extract data using the category
        # 3. append image_path to self.history
        # 4. return the extraction result
        pass

# Test it:
prompts = {
    "receipt": "Extract store, date, total.",
    "chart": "Describe axes and trends.",
    "screenshot": "Extract visible text."
}
pipe = ImagePipeline(prompts)

r1 = pipe.run("photos/grocery_receipt.png")
print(r1["category"])
print(r1["prompt_used"])
print(r1["output"])

r2 = pipe.run("imgs/sunset.jpg")
print(r2["category"])
print(len(pipe.history))
practice_challenges.expected_output:
receipt
Extract store, date, total.
Extracted receipt data from grocery_receipt.png
photo
2

en: In classify(), iterate over self.prompts.keys() and check if key.lower() is in image_path.lower() — return the first match or 'photo'
Use os.path.basename(image_path) or image_path.split('/')[-1] to get just the filename from a full path
run() simply calls self.classify() then self.extract() with the result, appends to history, and returns the extract dict

practice_challenges.test_code:
prompts = {"receipt": "Get total.", "chart": "Get trends.", "screenshot": "Get text."}
pipe = ImagePipeline(prompts)
assert pipe.history == []
r = pipe.run("docs/receipt_march.png")
assert r["category"] == "receipt"
assert r["prompt_used"] == "Get total."
assert r["output"] == "Extracted receipt data from receipt_march.png"
assert pipe.history == ["docs/receipt_march.png"]
r2 = pipe.run("pics/dog.jpg")
assert r2["category"] == "photo"
assert r2["prompt_used"] == "Describe the image."
assert r2["output"] == "Extracted photo data from dog.jpg"
assert len(pipe.history) == 2
r3 = pipe.run("data/sales_chart.png")
assert r3["category"] == "chart"
assert r3["prompt_used"] == "Get trends."
c = pipe.classify("Screenshot_2026.png")
assert c == "screenshot"

instruction.en: Build a `PromptRouter` class that selects the best system prompt for a visual task based on keywords in the user's question. On init, accept a list of route dicts, each with 'keywords' (list of strings) and 'prompt' (string). Implement `match(question)` that returns the prompt of the first route where ANY keyword appears in the question (case-insensitive), or a default prompt 'Describe what you see.' if no route matches. Implement `route(question, image_path)` that returns a dict with 'question', 'image_path', 'matched_prompt', and 'routed' (True if a route matched, False if default was used). Track the count of routed vs default calls in `stats` dict with keys 'routed' and 'default'.

practice_challenges.starter_code:
class PromptRouter:
    def __init__(self, routes: list):
        # Store routes and initialize stats = {'routed': 0, 'default': 0}
        pass

    def match(self, question: str) -> str:
        # Check each route: if any keyword in route['keywords']
        # appears in question (case-insensitive), return route['prompt']
        # If no match, return 'Describe what you see.'
        pass

    def route(self, question: str, image_path: str) -> dict:
        # 1. Call self.match(question)
        # 2. Determine if it was routed or default
        # 3. Update self.stats accordingly
        # 4. Return dict with question, image_path, matched_prompt, routed
        pass

# Test it:
routes = [
    {"keywords": ["price", "cost", "total"], "prompt": "Extract all prices and totals."},
    {"keywords": ["text", "read", "ocr"], "prompt": "Extract all visible text."},
    {"keywords": ["trend", "graph", "chart"], "prompt": "Analyze the chart data."},
]
router = PromptRouter(routes)

r1 = router.route("What is the total cost?", "receipt.jpg")
print(r1["matched_prompt"])
print(r1["routed"])

r2 = router.route("Is this a cat?", "cat.jpg")
print(r2["matched_prompt"])
print(r2["routed"])

print(router.stats)
practice_challenges.expected_output:
Extract all prices and totals.
True
Describe what you see.
False
{'routed': 1, 'default': 1}

en: In match(), convert question to lowercase once, then for each route check if any(kw.lower() in question_lower for kw in route['keywords'])
The default prompt is always 'Describe what you see.' — compare match() result to this string to determine if routed is True or False
In route(), after calling match(), increment stats['routed'] if a route matched or stats['default'] if the default was used

practice_challenges.test_code:
routes = [
    {"keywords": ["price", "cost"], "prompt": "Get prices."},
    {"keywords": ["text", "read"], "prompt": "Get text."},
]
router = PromptRouter(routes)
assert router.stats == {"routed": 0, "default": 0}
r = router.route("What is the price?", "img.jpg")
assert r["matched_prompt"] == "Get prices."
assert r["routed"] == True
assert r["question"] == "What is the price?"
assert r["image_path"] == "img.jpg"
assert router.stats["routed"] == 1
r2 = router.route("How old is this?", "x.jpg")
assert r2["matched_prompt"] == "Describe what you see."
assert r2["routed"] == False
assert router.stats["default"] == 1
r3 = router.route("Can you READ this sign?", "sign.jpg")
assert r3["matched_prompt"] == "Get text."
assert r3["routed"] == True
assert router.stats["routed"] == 2
m = router.match("Show me the COST")
assert m == "Get prices."
